turns.content: Why does my Rust GUI app suddenly lose the ability to paste images from Windows when I switch to Linux, even though I'm using the same logic? I thought arboard was supposed to handle everything seamlessly across platforms so why is it failing on one side while the other works fine?

The confusion comes from assuming arboard's implementation automatically mirrors every OS clipboard format without checking. On Windows, arboard relies on the Windows Registry and the standard Win32 Clipboard Data structure to expose formats like rich text or images, whereas Linux uses X11 selection protocols where image data is often stored as a specific atom like 'image/png' rather than being directly accessible through the generic clipboard query. Your app likely only queries for basic string types via arboard::Clipboard::get_text, which works on both systems but silently ignores image formats until you explicitly request them using arboard::Image or by iterating over available formats with arboard::AvailableFormats to see what's actually supported.
I'm trying to build a simple cross-platform app so I can read text from the clipboard on Windows and Mac using Rust. Does the arboard crate work out of the box without any extra dependencies?

The arboard crate provides native OS integration for clipboard access, but it does require linking against the system libraries specific to your platform like windows-core or objc2-objc for macOS. You cannot use it in a standalone binary without these bindings because arboard relies on accessing the operating system's clipboard API directly.
Oh okay so I need to add those extra crates first. Once I have them, can I just call some function to get the text from the clipboard and then write my own string back?

Yes, once you add the platform bindings as dependencies in your Cargo.toml, you can use arboard's CopyText trait to retrieve or store data. The crate exposes methods like copy_text() which returns a String or Result type depending on whether the clipboard actually contains text.
Wait, so does that mean I don't need to write any code at all? Just call the function and it handles everything?

That's not quite right. While arboard simplifies the complexity of cross-platform clipboard logic significantly, you still need to write Rust code to instantiate a Clipboard instance, invoke the copy or paste methods, and handle the Result type if the clipboard is empty or contains an image instead of text.
